commentBody: I've been solving the Sunday Crossword for more than 50 years. I've enjoyed tricky themes and simple themes (and tolerated no themes). I've enjoyed fill clues of varying difficulty. I expect some Sundays to be easier, and some to be harder, and I'm fine with that. I thought today's theme was  clever, and I thank Lance and John for creating the puzzle. I also thought it was, for lack of a better description, a Monday-level theme, and I found most of the fill clues to be Monday-level. To me, this was not the Sunday Crossword, it was a large Monday. YMMV.
Agreed on ease. My best ever Sunday solve time by over 5 minutes. Still fun.
I enjoyed the puzzle, but it was easy for a Sunday. I finished ten minutes faster than my previous Sunday best.
Lovely that the initial word of all the theme answers is actually the *initial* word.
Pretty easy but still enjoyable.
Was a fun Sunday puzzle, enjoyed the aha moment after 22A: HELEN OF TROY was filled in, which was also my favorite clue of the set (HOT woman worth fighting for).
VIP- Very Interesting Puzzle. Loved this puzzle a lot. Very refreshing!
The clue for KEEP IT DOWN seemed unnecessarily dark and morbid to me until I read the column.
Under 8 minutes on a Sunday for the first time in years. That said, it was enjoyable (I love the theme). Could have just been a skosh harder.

Got stuck on "Kind of hike". 

Groaned audibly when I finally got it.

I was amazed at the list of great theme entries in the constructor notes. And yet they seem hard to come up with! Looked at the rest of the puzzle for inspiration…

TB in cats?   TOE BEANS

MAD permission system?  MOM AND DAD

MR Johnson (my partner)?  MUMBLE RAPPER
{just kidding about the rapping, but the man has the mumbling down to an art.}

“Hells Corner” the F-Sat-Sun of last weekend had me thinking there was an uptick in difficulty, if only temporarily. But this week, all 7 days were well below average times . Not a criticism just an observation. That being said, I can’t imagine the time it takes to construct a puzzle. I’m just grateful to get one loaded on the app every day.
Too easy
AID in Poland?

Random thoughts

I wouldn't go as far as Barry in saying the cluing was Monday level. I'll say Tuesday, but my super power is trivia, so I'm not sure how much of an advantage that gave me. But I give myself a headslap for not getting ORCAS until the very end.

Will we see @Andrzej? It's only half a Kugelman. But it's as if by invoking him three times ala Beetlejuice, Andrzej summoned him. 

Like Caitlin, GOT evokes Game of Thrones for me. The column's photo and caption cracked me up.

Unlike Caitlin, as the daughter of a NY Giants fan of a certain era, knowing who Too Tall Jones  was a gimme.

That's all for me for now. 
Have some MADEIRA, m'dear.
Pause while I do a search for Socialite Sedgwick.

Well, you had me at HELENOFTROY in terms of fun factor!
Look, I agree with Barry, Sundays ain’t what they used to be but I’m getting beyond expecting much more (I’m still going to harp on Thursdays and Saturdays). 
I’m going to be content with an engaging, clever, consistent theme—check, although more impressed by the ones whose acronym was more inherent— so not as wild about THE Big Game or GOT in the ballpark. Loved the rest though, and just going to brag I somehow got SNAKESONAPLANE immediately with no crosses, how weird is that? 
Yeah, a lot of easy answers but enough interesting accessory fill and clever clues to still amuse me. PARADIDDLE! MUMBLERAPPERS! (I don’t speak much Spanish but I imagine, regardless, Bad Bunny’s picture is in that encyclopedia entry). And clever clues for REV, NEWDO, even EPEE—so, check, check.

Probably too many proper nouns for some but I think all gettable from crosses—my history teacher will be happy to know I got GHENT and TRENT pretty quickly. Overall a more than decent grid—so, check. 

No complaints then. I’ll be holding my breath for the occasional really scintillating Sunday like we got with Middlemarch and Roubdabouts, but in the meantime will be more than satisfied with a reasonably amusing Sunday like today.
Cute premise. Very easy. I enjoyed seeing the constructors' rejected clues!

Very fun theme, even if, as others have groused, not very challenging.

I comment solely to thank Caitlin for the cover image. (Took me a sec to see that it related to the GOT in the ballpark clue!) I have never, nor will I ever in the future, love a sports franchise as much as I do the Kansas City Royals.  So imagine my glee at seeing them honored this way.  

The Boys Are Playing Some Ball!*


*(not really so far this year, but hope springs eternal)
29:11, a personal best, which means these are getting too easy. HOT was a dead giveaway, though I really wanted KID to be KEEPTHEKID. TOTH to Red Cloud.

Well, no relief for those who hunger for a mind-bending Sunday puzzle.

No real relief for me, either, as I had some pretty scary blanks for a long while in the final moments of my 45 minute solve, and was hoping others would have found it challenging, too.

No relief for the families of the hundreds of thousands who starved to death or died of easily treatable diseases because of the Muskification of USAID.  Hey, not our problem.  AmIRite?

Niels Bohr is one of my heroes, because the Bohr Hydrogen atom was the first "quantum mechanics" is could understand.  Too bad the Bohr H atom was factually incorrect in terms of angular momentum, and a dead end model for future quantum investigation into atoms.  

On the other hand, all of physics runs into problems when we have three or more interacting bodies.  

Of course even two body problems are unsolvable if we include relativistic effects.  

And one body problems are also unsolvable if we consider self-energy.

But we're good with zero-body problems, right?

Nope.  The zero-body problem is also unsolvable because we can't perfectly describe vacuum polarization.

I think I've sown enough discord for the moment.

"we were both pretty sad to have to leave [Air BUD] for BLOW UP DOLL"

Oh, even better. Can we ditch some of this sexism please?
Clever cluing, interesting wordplay. Line of latitude? Switch positions? Bad lighting? That's what I come here for. Quick solve at 13:11, but I'm not complaining.

At least for me, the quote in 19A (with answer AFOOT) is associated with Sherlock Holmes.  Checking XWord Info, it's been clued with a reference to the detective six times since Will Shortz took over as editor.  Today's clue is the first to refer to its appearance in the Shakespeare play.
My Diary of a Crossword Fiend Review: https://crosswordfiend.com/2026/04/11/sunday-april-12-2026/#ny

I don't rush through puzzles, I try to relax, and sometimes I'm doing something else at the same time. Nevertheless, this was definitely on the easier side, although I got hung up on BRIaR instead of BRIER and also on some of the TV/movie characters, as I'm not a watcher, but I managed to get the answers from the crossings. A faster time than usual, but not near my fastest. It was a lot of fun, though the trick was found with HELEN OF TROY, but there was still some resistance with some of the theme answers. I was surprised to come up with SoaP as quickly as I did.

Thanks, Lance and John!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Alternate cluing for 78D:  What your heart may do as you contemplate the direction in which the New York Times crossword puzzles are heading

I suppose some of you will object to the length of the clue.

Give it a few more months.

I spent this whole puzzle in déjà vu thinking where did I just see that...
GHENT, STENT, and TRENT 
EPEE, EDIE, IDED, and IDIG
ASTER and ESTER
ARARAT on top of STRAT 
PITBOSS and PITAS sharing the P, just up from KEEPITDOWN
ONIT, FIXIT, ILLALLOWIT
AOL, OLA, ALOE, and ANON
FIDE and FIE

Initialisms were cute, but were hit or miss. HOT, GOT, BOB, and KID yes. THE marginal. SOAP not so much. 

Was fast ... up until flyspecking BRIAR / BRIER that oh so Naticky crossed a Portuguese wine.
11 seconds off my personal best, pretty quick one after a tough saturday
BRIER hasn't been a valid spelling for centuries. But since someone somewhere spelled it that way before, ILLALOWIT.
Kind of wish that I would've saved this one for Sunday morning.
Super easy for a Sunday. Finished in about 12 seconds with my eyes closed. Kinda difficult to tap Reveal Puzzle with one's eyes closed, but I did not give up! Pure muscle memory.

So, less tounge in cheek, I had to research why fie and egad are mild oaths. I have never been taught that meaning of oath. Oaths are like promising not to lie in court or allegiance to a group or the law.
Now I learn they are also mild explitives. 
As I kid, I wondered why what I always called cursing others called swearing. This makes sense when cursing is also thought of as swearing an oath.
The connection is still a little hazy to me. Seems to be tied to culture of centuries ago when a phrase that used the name of God was by nature considered an oath, while at the same time being sinful. By only alluding to God by changing the pronunciation a bit, the oath was acceptable.
The culture of NYT crossword solving will take many years of study.
While I appreciated the musical refs (arco, Strat, paradiddle), overall this was way too easy. Finished before finishing my coffee.

MUMBLE RAPPERS ugh. Thank the pandemic for ending that era. Industry sobered up and realized it wasn’t a vibe, and quarantine forced forward creativity. Young Thug is alright, Jeffery was ahead of its time. 

ARGENTINIA was a gimme and got me excited about Speed’s World Cup song againia!!!!
So easy! And that’s without the clues giving actual hints. Looking forward to a tricky Sunday puzzle some day…

brier got me. thought madaira might be wrong but was pretty sure briar was right so didn't find the error

quick and mostly quite easy to enter all squares but that one correctly. yet I did not complete the puzzle unaided so in that sense it was not easy at all. sundays usually feel like a bit of a slog, maybe due to solving on a small screen
They had me at Helen of Troy and Keep It Down.😊I liked the theme.Enough clever word play to keep me engaged.
Easy? Well, easy when you know how! It was hard until i got the gimmick. Then it fell into place. But at first it was “huh”? A worthy and excellent Sunday, in my view.

I thought this was really fun. A decent Sunday effort, plus the theme was clever. Snakes On A Plane and Mail-Order Bride made me laugh out loud. Also had BRIaR, but I'm familiar with Madeira, more as a location than a wine. And the BOB clue was super clever, imo. Above all, I liked the clever coincidences that they found with the theme clues.

Maybe I got impatient - perhaps because I was solving most of this around 4:30 am 😞 - but I couldn’t finish this without a bunch of lookups. I cruised through the top half thinking it would be a personal best, and then I hit a wall.

Also, the theme didn’t seem consistent to me. What’s the connection between MAIL ORDER BRIDES and mob wives (64A)? I get that HELEN OF TROY (22A) is HOT because she was good-looking, but then TEXAS HOLD EM is THE? And 28A [GOT in the ballpark?] seems like quite a stretch (and yes, I read the column). It still seems weak to me.
BRIER crossed with MADEIRA was gross, along with a decent number of other nouns/names/obscura (many crossing!). Theme was just fun enough to even out to a solid average
Took me a little while to get the theme, but I did have a few glasses of wine before I started....
“Brier” patch? Huh?

A thoroughly enjoyable Sunday. Yes, on the easier side but with enough misdirects to make it interesting. The HIT woman gave me my way in, SOAP made me chuckle, despite never having seen the film, only Samuel L Jackson’s none emu friendly memes. MOB was the one I had most difficulty with.
The E in BRIER threw me for a while. I knew MADEIRA was right, so looked for another prickly plant that would fit. The dark green clue made me laugh. I went through every hue I could think of before realising it was the veg. Ditto 96A, another fun misdirect.

All in all, while not being a major workout I still felt it was Sunday worthy. Not every weekend puzzle needs to melt the brain.

Wow, look at these themers:
•	The clues begin with an all-cap word.  
•	Those initial words are *initial* words, initialisms made up of the answer’s first letters.
•	The rest of the clue defines the answer.  Thus, BLONDE ON BLONDE is a “Dylan album”, GIVE OR TAKE is the meaning of “in the ballpark”, and so on.

What a wild wordplay web each themer spins!

I love wordplay and our quirky language, and this theme hit my happy button.  As did the sing-song PARADIDDLE.  And get this! – I noticed that the answer DAMON backward is NOMAD, went down a small rabbit hole and was reminded that the actor played a wandering spy in the “Bourne” franchise, then further found out that in 2023 he had NOMAD tattooed on his arm!

Two original clues stood out to me – The very clever [Line of latitude?] for I’LL ALLOW IT, and the highly misdirecting [Dark green] for KALE.

Lance and John, I imagine that you took great pleasure in coming up with these themers, and so did I in unpeeling them.  Thank you!
After years of crosswords, I think I finally got the message.  I need to take up fencing

I thought it was a pretty good puzzle with a fun simple theme.  BLONDE ON BLONDE is one of my favorite albums.  A few bones to pick.

BRIAR vs BRIER I didn’t like.  Using a less common archaic spelling crossed with a word like MADEIRA when if you don’t know the trivia about wine MEDAIRA looks fine.  That was the last square I figured out.

Also asking the name of a Baywatch actress I hated.  Asking about modern pop culture you can at least kind of follow just by keeping an eye on social media feeds.  A show from 30 years ago that existed to show skin?  Not something crossword solvers should be expected to know.

The rest of the cluing was fine, maybe a few too many proper nouns.

I liked this one. Clever quirky theme, and interesting fill. 

Regarding AFOOT, I associate it with Leonard Cohen -- as in "God is alive, magic is afoot". A day after his death was announced in November 2016, someone made a sign with those words and placed it among the many flowers left in front of his home in Little Portugal, in Montreal. Comforting words for a dark time.

My dad showed up this morning.

To be fair, 
he is *always* sitting next to me
as I solve
peering over my shoulder
and marveling like a proud papa does, 
cheering me on 
for being *far* more clever
than I actually am. 

But today I heard him chuckle
and say,

“I eat my peas with honey.
I’ve done it all my life.
It may seem kind of funny,
but it keeps them on the knife.”

He found it hilarious,
for the millionth time.

So, that was cool.

When I saw John Kugelman's name on this puzzle, I knew that Andrjez would not be among the commenters. I also knew that I wouldn't particularly like this puzzle. Sorry to say that nothing about it disabused me of my prediction. I found it to be not only Monday-Tuesday easy, but an overall lightweight and ho-hum theme. Yes, a couple of the theme answers were cute, e.g. HELEN OF TROY, but instantly gettable. And a few were a little harder. But over all, I found it a big snoozefest. I also didn't appreciate having to see USAID, when it's nowhere to be found where it should be.
I'm reminded of an old joke: Amount of beauty required to launch 1 ship? 1 millihelen.

For me, a rather forgettable Sunday puzzle. The grid was large. The fill was OK. The theme was weak. EDIE wasn't Brickell or Falco.  Someone forgot the exclamation point on "What a mess", at least in the online version of the puzzle. 

Easy to solve (about 30 minutes), but unsatisfying (to me).

Yesterday I complained that there were a few too many gimmes  (for me) in a Saturday puzzle.  @Andrzej challenged me on it, and I (foolishly) listed them.

If I were to list the gimmes in today's puzzle, I'd be typing until tomorrow's puzzle drops.

"Now I long for yesterday."

Regarding BRIER. 

Many of us who spend time in the woods are quite familiar with  plants of the genus Smilax, which, per Wikipedia, "will grow as shrubs, forming dense impenetrable thickets. They will also grow over trees and other plants up to 10 m high, their hooked thorns allowing them to hang onto and scramble over branches."

Members of this genus are commonly called "greenbriers", at least here in eastern North America. 

I go to great lengths to avoid these BRIER patches.

(In the puzzle, I entered BRIAR, but switched immediately to BRIER when MADEIRA was required. No reason to complain about this element of the puzzle.)

Things I liked about today's puzzle:

The clue "Line of latitude" for I'LL ALLOW IT.
"Dad's deflection" for ASK MOM.

Anything else?

Yes!   Best was the photo accompanying today's Wordplay column.  It really made me think.  "What is the Kansas City mascot doing wrestling a bunch of cosplaying Ren-Fairers?  Wait. That chair!  It's Game of Thrones!"
I GOT it!
GoT in the ballpark!!!
this took me almost an hour to complete but proud to get gold eventually. BRIER tripped me up for a while- not sure we get this in the UK?
Lost gold when I had to autocheck to find MISCUE. Those Turkish crosses were brutal. Massive fail on my part, but dissapointment is excellent sauce for a delucious mimosa! Cheers.
It’s a game! Why people get so upset and feel like The Crossword needs to be custom-built according to their own ways of thinking and personal preferences is truly beyond me. Have some fun, will ya?

Once you cotton on to the trick, it's all over....not that I didn't enjoy some of the clever clues!  

I would have liked Ed Jones's nickname to be SHORTY, but the space was too long.  Missed opportunity, there, guys.

"Trading card error" for MISCUT?  Hardly the stuff of tragedy!  
A mistake using one's rotary cutter on fabric that cost over $12/yard-- now, THAT is serious.

"You can't hurt me from over there."

"Shuriken."

Well... not all that easy for me of course, but managed to work it out. An enjoyable solve and a cute theme. And of course my puzzle find today. A Sunday from June 20, 2004 by Manny Nosowsky with the title "Pack of 20." Some theme answers: DANRATHER MOUNTARARAT DIXIECRATS FORATIME UNGRATEFUL ILLITERATES FRATERNITYHOUSE SPOILEDBRAT STRATAGEM And a bunch of others. And the 'reveal': "Leader of the pack hidden in this puzzle?" THEPIEDPIPEROFHAMELIN And yes, there were 20 RATs in the puzzle. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/20/2004&g=29&d=A ....

I’m here to protest all the DEEPFAKE content in today’s puzzle and column. The “cat” in that photo has a head which, if you measure carefully, is out of proportion to the rest of its body. Plus, the Flying Nun obviously never actually appeared in public with Robin Hood.

Finally, though the puzzle “appears” Sunday-sized, that is merely a trick of the light. I was able to solve it using only seven fingers on each hand, which made for a fast, if sloppy, solve. Sure, I had fun, but I’m just an AI bot. I feel “sorry” for you human commenters - you know who you are.
agreed, fairly easy...tuesday/wednesday-ish..but a pleasant (albeit brief) meditation on a quiet rainy sunday morning nevertheless.

I adored the theme, which felt completely fresh and was very imaginative.  All of the themers were interesting and well-chosen -- and some seemed especially inspired.  HELEN OF TROY for the HOT woman (she must have been pretty darned hot, right?) was a real find.  But MAIL ORDER BRIDES for MOB bosses may be even better.  What fun this was to solve -- and the only thing I can think of that would have been more fun would be to have brainstormed it.

Also, note how lively the cluing is.  Interesting ways to clue ANON; PEAS, COOKS and NEW DO, for example.  "Fresh cut" for NEW DO stumped me completely until I pretty much had every cross.

This will go on my running list for Sunday POY -- though I'm pretty sure there was a Sunday that I liked even more.  Being me, though, I don't remember what it was.  Why do you think I keep a running list?  Anyway, great job, Lance and John.  I really enjoyed this.

The theme was fun, but the fill was just. so. easy. Lots of gimmes, like "The game's _____" and "East of ____." It was missing the sort of clever cluing that makes a puzzle fun. Puzzles should be puzzling.

This puzzle needed to LEVEL UP.
Really enjoyed this straightforward Sunday and was charmed by the theme, HELEN OF TROY being a HOTtie made me giggle.

When I thought I had finished this I realized I had a BONER, I stared at it for ages but whatever I tried it wouldn't fit. 

One of the reason I like Sundays is that I don't try to beat a particular time so there is no rush and I was able to just play with different combinations until it all came together. 

Very enjoyable crossword, even for those across the pond.
Struggled with 112A.  Finally got it and realized I am wearing their jersey!  Is there a term for when a clue to an answer is staring you in the face?
Good fun.  Also love the idea of "pick-your-own" themers.

Never heard of a 'LIE-IN' protest. Started with  sit-in, changed to die-in. But I remember Too Tall Jones.
I actually wasn't sure about the spelling of BRIxR, left a square blank, but knew MADEIRA. YMMV.
Fastest Sunday ever for me, but I liked it fine.

Fill felt very specific 
Lotta church.
Lotta Spanish.
Lotta millennial pop culture
Lotta near repeats. 

Meh
Treaty of GHENT and Council of TRENT? Nice historical pairing.
An easy Sunday but I enjoy solving the long answers and TOEBEAN was my favorite answer…would make a great cross in a puzzle with BOOP! Have a great Sunday and week ahead.

It was fun to solve and I enjoyed seeing GHENT and TRENT. It did go really fast. In fact I got each of the themers, in order, during my second pass through the across clues and never had to revisit the downs. But that was because the non-theme cluing was so straightforward. I quite enjoyed the theme entries and clues, but the rest of the cluing lacked misdirects, which would have provided more to chew on.

An issue that concerns me is that if we have long stretches of easy puzzles, then when a nice chewy one does come along, newer puzzlers are faced with letting go of long streaks. I could imagine that would be very anti-motivating. Back around 2017, after several years of solving, I didn't expect to be able to maintain a streak, and so when I couldn't get a puzzle, it was an engrossing learning experience rather than being devastating.
I’m obviously from a different generation than Ms. Lovenger as Too Tall just jumped out at me.

Today’s NYT Magazine “On Language” column discusses the rise in Silicon Valley of the use of the word “agentic.”  What an entrancing word!  I hope an agentic constructor somewhere out there will use it in a crossword puzzle soon.

Fun theme. Not difficult for me, which I define as not even considering doing a look-up. Was on the phone with my granddaughter, so can't really count the time. 
Never heard of the Kon Mari system. I just keep my house clean.

I don't usually complain about easiness, but thay was too easy. It felt like being on autopilot. It was the fastest I've understood a Sunday or Thursday theme in a long time. Having the initials made the theme fill very straightforward. It was clever, just better on another day. My only problem in solving was BRIER. MADAIRA looked wrong, but so did BRIER. I see now it's a secondary spelling.

Nice, mild Sunday puzzle. Finished without lookups about 20 minutes faster than usual. 

Rating:
7/10

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle (a/across clues ; d/down clues; a/ answers)

a/ little can be written of time’s children
d/ of the disappearing disappearing
a cat’s paw bath
a/ a soak
a laugh

Count me in the crowd getting stumped by BRIER instead of BRIAR, I didn't know the crossing answer MADEIRA vs MADAIRA so I finally had to look up the answers to track down my mistake. Oh well, it was fun aside from that - I always enjoy the puzzle themes that give you a little extra core for letters that will appear in the answers, even if they generally wind up on the easier end.

Another late puzzle find. A Sunday from January 29, 2023 by Rich Katz with the title: "The Final Frontier." Some theme clues and answers: "Embarrassing pants mishap?" SEATRIP "Shipper of British toilets?" LOOMOVER "Big fight for a Disney heroine from Polynesia?" MOANABOUT "Shortage of slime?" GOODEARTH "Painting of potatoes, e.g.?" STARCHART "Main course featuring Egyptian snake meat?" ASPENTREE And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/29/2023&g=77&d=D ...

It's a pleasure to solve a moderately challenging puzzle like today's, and learning something I didn't know before is icing on the cake. Until today I would have sworn that PARADIDDLE was a word invented by Dave Brubeck back in the cool 1960s. His song "Paradiddle Joe", performed here by the Brubeck Quartet with soloist Carmen McRae, concludes with the question answered in the clue to 74 Down. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xf2NzwN93hU
Trying to remember the last time I saw a Russian "czar".
Can someone explain breathers as LULLS.  I don’t get it.
Congratulations to Erik Agard, winner of the 2026 American Crossword Puzzle Tournament!

"Initial thoughts" after solve: there were some knotty areas and lively fill, that it seemed heavy with gaming terms (video & poker) giving it a certain bro-y middle school vibe, enhanced by boners, ninja star, Snakes on a Plane, etc. It made me wonder whether, in addition to a "leveling down" the difficulty that Barry and others have reported, the Times also seeks to gear their puzzles to what they perceive as the interests of Gen Z.

I'm unapologetically semi-woke, which means MAIL ORDER BRIDES slightly irked me. I know all's fair in love and crosswords, but I find the term pejorative-adjacent: lots of those brides are victims of trafficking, of abuse, of being put into terrible situations, and the term reduces all their dignity, humanity and individuality to a dismissively commoditized status. I'm surely over-reacting, a common symptom of the semi-woke, but can't shake the feeling that, given a choice, I would prefer NOT to see the term in my Sunday solve. 

It did make me muse on whether Helen of Troy is a classical example of the complexities of being a trafficked bride in a foreign land. She was also highly commodified (valued in various ways for her beauty, her noble status, wealth, etc.), but some accounts say she was abducted, others that she and Paris eloped, so whether she had "agency" is up for debate. Ask Achilles: he knew what was AFOOT (!).
This was fun and I thoroughly enjoyed it!
Nice fun Sunday and pretty easy. Once I got the “initial” clue, everything fell into place
I've never seen that spelling of a prickly bush before. Only took a stab at correcting it when I realized the Portuguese wine was misspelled.
Baddddd use of “cooks.” One can cook without being dishonest.
PARADIDLE used quite rightly. Props for correctly identifying it as a rudiment (There are 25 more).
Can someone explain to me why 'King's title, in brief' can give REV? Thanks.
Fun, fun, fun especially for a Sunday puzzle.
I uh… I don’t think that means “idiot mistake”. Or if it does I need to rethink every single relationship I’ve ever had.
Far be it from me to be that guy, but aren’t PEAS technically legumes, not vegetables?

One more puzzle find. I'll definitely repeat this tomorrow. A Sunday from August 3, 2008 by Peter A. Collins and Joe Krozel with the title: "Off with their Heads!" A couple of the answers embedded in their clues: "Will the long-winded REVERENDEVEREND his sermon?" "The majority of British HISTORYISTORY policy coming to fruition." "I noticed you use the SPOTLESSPOTLESS than the tarnished one." "The driver's crew decided to make the PITSTOPITSTOP priority" "The judges put the names or each FINALISTINALIST for the M.C. to read." And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/3/2008&g=23&d=A ...

I usually tend to read all comments, replying along the way, until i get through them all and then I give my comments if I have anything to add. By now the Monday puzzle has been out for 50 minutes, so I don't know if anyone will see this. I may need to rethink my model.

I thought the theme was pretty fun. I caught on with BLONDE ON BLONDE, and circled back to pick up the others. But I did think a lot of the other fill and especially the clueing was far too easy for a Sunday. Not all of it, but a lot. And I agree that the responsibility lies with the editors, not the constructors. And I agree with those who suggest that the editors have likely been pressured by management to make things easier to increase revenue. And I lament our loss. At this point I pretty much breeze through M-W, so I really look forward to Thu-Sun.  I never hate the puzzles, but I find myself disappointed of late.

Very easy, a new record for me (about 40%of my average!). Didn’t help that the app now gives away the title when entering into the puzzle, it’s more fun to figure it out from scratch imo. 

Also, it’s currently 7:30 PM on Sunday, why has the entire week’s times been erased already?
I thought this was going to be my first 100% unassisted Sunday puzzle, but I ended up having to check the key to find a typo. Maybe next week.

theme was easy enough. this puzzle had some really fun clues, loved MUMBLERAPPERS, ASKMOM, NINJASTAR, etc. Found some of the clues veryyyy niche trivia, which is challenging to parse when the across and the down is random historical facts and/or Turkish trivia. I'm sure there's a turkish historian somewhere out there that enjoyed it immensely, lol.
MADEIRA crossing BRIER was pretty rough, I have to admit.

@Barry Ancona I agree. I found myself wondering if they could govern two sets of clues, as they do for Fridays. I’m all for getting more people doing crosswords but not at the expense of a good challenge! Thank goodness I still have a way to go in the archive.
Red that as “give us” two sets of clues… good ol’ autocorrect!

@Barry Ancona. For me, scaling my today’s time by the ratio of areas, today’s was about a Wednesday.  I kinda enjoy the 2-hour Saturdays but they are a bit over the top.

I can’t imagine doing any Sunday in 6 minutes though.  My fingers are just too slow and fat.

But you experienced solvers are amazing.  I have good recall now for many of the more common crossword words like EPEE, ACELA, ORCA, TSAR, ALOE, etc, but the particulars in ASSIDI, ENDS not AIMS, etc, can get me still.  Your experience is way beyond mine and very impressive.

But I don’t think MUMBLERAPPERS, PARADIDDLE, and ROCCO are Monday level.

@Barry Ancona
Thank you for posting the news about Bob Panoff--I haven't been reading Wordplay much recently, so I was glad that the information filtered down to me. Bob was a character, and I'm glad I had to opportunity to know him. The last few years, he gauged his health by how well and quickly he could solve a NYT crossword. Thanks again.

@sonnel

There’s an interesting distinction here between the quality and freshness of the fill on the one hand, and the difficulty of the clues on the other. 

MUMBLERAPPERS and PARADIDDLE are indeed great entries. I would say this puzzle had a number of others — just in the midlength entries there were PITBOSS, DEEPFAKES, TOEBEAN, and NINJASTAR. 

But what would have made it more Sunday-like (i.e., a large Wednesday or ideally Thursday) would have been harder cluing, especially on the entries crossing the themers.

sonnel,
(1) Doing a Sunday in 6 minutes? You talkin' to me? I'm no speed solver, and I use a stylus to cut a stone tablet, so I take my time. An easy puzzle goes quickly for me, but I don't break any speed limits.
(2) I said I found most -- not all -- of the fill clues to be Monday-level. There were one or two answers -- proper nouns -- I didn't know from the clues, as is often the case with a Monday puzzle. They had Monday crosses.

Barry Ancona,

SLAM today’s puzzle difficulty?
SOLVED LIKE A MONDAY

NYT archives assessment?
NOW YOU’RE TALKING

@Barry Ancona
Might quibble on details, and will post after I've read comments, but in general agree. Solved in 20 minutes less than my Sunday average.

@Barry Ancona I have noted, but not commented, on the ongoing discussions about end-of-week puzzles being too easy. But it definitely seems to me to be an accelerating trend. Although I can't tell how much of it is that the puzzles are becoming easier, and how much of it is because I've been working the puzzles on and off siince the late 1970s, and have worked almost 4200 on this platform. But I am working my way back through the archives(I'm back to the pre-Trump, we never imagined we'd be here days of 2014), and it does seem to me that the older puzzles were more challenging, even given the dated cultural references.

Lewis,
Say again, please.

@Barry -- Thank you, as this made no sense at all!

It should have read: Lovely that the initial word of all the theme CLUES is actually the *initial* word.

Read you five by now, Lewis. 
Question, though: If not for what you call "lovely," is there actually a theme?

@Rahul

That the clue was two words rather than one made it a little easier to avoid the darker implications.

@Sam
That was totally fair, even though it was my last fill and it took an alphabet run to get it.

@Cat Lady Margaret

I bet I can out-mumble Mr. Johnson. I've certainly been practicing long enough,

@Vaer 
Oy, as a kid I never realized how creepy the Madeira song is. I just liked listening to the chorus.

@Vaer

Yes, I too only knew that one line about Madeira, and the puzzle made me go look.
Yikes!
"Baby It's Cold Outside" times 100!
@Laura Edie was a local here in the Santa Barbara area… look up the Sedgwick Reserve.
@Laura: There was even a movie (of sorts) made about her. Factory Girl (2006).

@Laura

She was best known for being part of Andy Warhol’s Factory group. Would-be actress, model, drug addict who died young. From a wealthy Massachusetts family, her relatives include actress Kyra Sedgwick (cousin).
@Francis Is it still sowing if it's not something worth reaping?

@Francis

You can’t sow discord if people have no idea what you’re talking about.

@Scott

I hope so, because most of the things I sow are not worth reaping.

@Steve L

Still working on that part.

@Francis. Despite the discord  - most current scientific advancement stem from Bohr's quantum model of the atom.  In "Copenhagen," the excellent play about the meeting between the Jewish physicist Bohr and his former top student Heisenberg - then working to develop the atomic bomb for Germany in 1941 -  there is one magical scene where they discuss the mysterious quantum atom in a very accessible manner.  Highly recommend the movie, which is based on the Michael Frayn play.

@Francis the extent to which the one body problem is unsolvable depends on your take on the zero body problem…. And thus the logical order should be inverted 0, 1, 2, many 😀 

No discord sown

@Dave

Good point.  I vastly under-discussed Bohr's absolute centrality of the birth of quantum physics.  It embarrasses me that I actually did that.  (Forgive me, Herr Dr. Bohr, if you can hear me.)

But I've often thought that the problematic Bohr atom, which was so impressive to me at the time, was an superb example of an utterly ingenious failure on the way to even more utterly ingenious success.

Moreover, it has tremendous educational value in introducing some of the early aspects of quantum mechanics.  (I mean it worked perfectly for the H atomic spectrum.)

@Ιασων

This may be a great opportunity for me.  I read that breakdown of the n-body problem in some offbeat section of a book on my research advisor's bookshelf.  

I always thought it was really clever, but worried that I was not only not remembering it right, but misremembering it in a way  that would embarrass me among physicists.  

So I may have just done that.

But does anyone know the source of that bit of writing?  I have a vague recollection of finding it somewhere online, but I have no idea where or how.

@Steve L

I understood it perfectly. Didn't you, Steve?

@Teresa

Clear as mud.
@Scott Sure - hence the biblical "sow the wind, reap the whirlwind."

@Francis chemists can only be embarrassed by other chemists. Your quip works in the abstract. We do routinely compute approximations to the 1, 2, and n-body problems.  

So it really depends on whether by solve you mean get a gold star. That, as I’m fairly sure you already know, only exists in mathematics. The rest of us just move along to the next question.

@Francis

The problem with the Bohr atomic model is that it is so darned easy to draw. Bohr himself quickly moved past it, along with the rest of the physics world. But a model that he used for less than a decade is still all over the place in textbooks (and everywhere else) a century later.

@Francis
At least, we're no longer kidding ourselves that USAID is an existing, functioning institution. 😔

@Dave Bohr is relentlessly quotable. Here is my current favourite: "The opposite of a correct statement is a false statement. But the opposite of a profound truth may well be another profound truth." Find bunches more here: https://www.goodreads.com/author/quotes/821936.Niels_Bohr
@Moira Are all blow-up dolls female? Or all buddies? Where's the sexism, exactly?
Air Bud is a dog.

@Moira

Air BUD doesn’t really work anyway. All the other themes have the definition encompassed in the non capitalized part of the clues. Air BUD might apply to Blow Up Doll but not Air by itself.

There are dolls of both (or all or no) sexes. 
Air Bud is a dog. Where is the sexism?

@SP
we can see from their list of those that didn't make it that they considered putting the initialisms both first and last.  But as it turns out all of the  themers started with the initialism, so Air BUD would have been an outlier.  That said, I think Air BUD matches other entries actually in the puzzle. i.e. HELEN OF TROY was a HOT woman worth fighting for, and a BLOW UP DOLL is an Air BUD.

@Scott

This is the sixth time that [Bad lighting] has been a clue for ARSON; [Switch positions] for ON/OFF has been used before, although the similar [Switch words] has been used more often. 

[Lines of latitude ?]. I’LL ALLOW IT. That’s a new one. 

If you solved this one in 13:11, you should be experienced enough to have seen the first two before.

@Eric Hougland

BTW, the above question was mild curiosity, not a challenge.

@Francis

I have nothing against Niels Bohrs. 

I just didn’t care for the Bohrs/Bores pun in the clue. 

Thanks for reading my review.

@Eric Hougland
I think you were too kind. Double meh for me.

@Eric Hougland
LOL just yesterday I told someone who *hasn't* seen Married to the MOB about the honeymoon suite with the banner over the bed which says Veni Veni Veni.
@Kevin A comment of moment.
@Kevin That vino combined with an archaic spelling cost me a *ten minute* PB

@Hugh 8 here. Super disappointing…again.

They’re bound and determined to drive away serious crossword puzzlers.

@John

Speak for yourself, I'm not going anywhere
@Isa internet says it's still legit but I for sure was not familiar and it cost me the solve haha
@Isa Merriam-Webster has it.  Of course, their idea of what constitutes English and mine are miles apart.

@MExpat

And which of the two has an army of lexicographers working full time to determine what constitutes English?

@Isa According to Google's ngram viewer, "brier patch" was still more common than "briar patch" as recently as 1920, and "brier patch" is still in use today even though it's outnumbered by "briar patch" by about a factor of 11. I'd say that makes it a minor but valid spelling.

@Isa
Up the big hill from Conway, AR, to the north of I-40, lies the village of GreenBRIER.  The whole reason for its existence is to refute your claim, so far as I can tell.  (I passed through Greenbrier to get to a mountain orchard where they grew Loring peaches...The Best Peaches In The World.)
Prescriptionism vs. Descriptivism yet again.
...or something like that
@Isa THANK YOU! I knew if I came here after an unsuccessful flyspecking session I would find my error. MADaIRA seemed a more reasonable alternate spelling than BRIER.

@Steve L
My guess would be "Neither."

@Isa Canada's male curlers still play for the BRIER trophy. For a report on this year's play -- won, I'm proud to say, by Manitoba's team -- go here: https://www.sportsnet.ca/curling/article/2026-montanas-brier-scores-standings-and-schedule/ Sgd., An ex-curler

@Charles Peterson

Dictionaries haven been prescriptive since BRIER was a normal spelling.

@Isa - What's amazing to me is that this posting, which begins with a demonstrably false statement, is the highest-ranked comment today. 

Oh, how we have slipped!

@John 

I highly recommend John McWhorter’s “Nine Nasty Words: English in the Gutter: Then, Now, and Forever.” It’s an interesting history of how what is considered to be offensive language has evolved over the centuries.

@Eric Hougland 
Thanks! Added it to my queue.

@John
I can speak to the difference between swearing and cursing. To curse is to wish evil on someone, send them to he11. Swearing is the use of taboo language to express strong emotion (I just stubbed my __ toe on that __ nail!). But then you get into different meanings of "swearing" and "oath," say in Shakespeare versus in court. And in practice, if I stub my toe, I might both swear and curse in the same sentence and the distinction gets pretty fuzzy.

@Eric Hougland
One of the most interesting parts of learning other languages is what they consider sacred AKA taboo. French has a whole bunch of swear words built around the church: sacré bleu, tabernacle, etc. In English translation, there's nothing cuss-y about any of them.
@jes ditto
@Becky COYG

commentBody:
@Cherry

I found the theme quite meh myself, but it is internally consistent.

MAIL ORDER BRIDES has the initials MOB which can be read as "mob" and, hence, "MOB brides" applies as a vaguely punny and not particularly funny clue.

GIVE OR TAKE...GOT...and when you've estimated a result, say, "give or take" or, approximately, is the same as "GOT in the ballpark."

See?

Do the same with the rest and you will find that the fill has all the initials specified in the clues and the fill make similarly uninspiring puns.

And that they are all weakly punny and entirely unfunny.

You call them "weak."

I think that's an understatement.

But they are consistently weak.

@Steve L
I think you’re right - it’s the HELEN OF TROY clue that made me think it was inconsistent. I thought they were trying to do that with all of them. I would have liked it better if they did. Meh.
@Joseph Have more Madeira, m'dear.-Noël Cowa4d
https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/brier
@Barry Ancona yes, and…

@pmom Agreed. This repeated misspelling that has worked its way into the dictionary was not my favorite. I put it in the same category as educated people using the pronunciation “processEEZ”: Correct now only because it’s been misused so much during my lifetime, but shouldn’t rhyme with indices.
@McX It's not a misspelling that worked its way into the dictionary; it is an older spelling that now may seem archaic.
@McX Language changes.

@McX
did you ngram it, as some others did? It was the more popular spelling from approx 1830-1910, and for the most part has been only a bit less relatively common since--perhaps a good bit less but not a rout.

@Lewis
I always preferred Flamadiddles...

@Mean Old Lady
Indeed!!! I wasn't very proficient but they were a lot of fun to play. I wonder how many times it's been in the puzzle....
@Getting Better or eating OREOs
@Getting Better or swimming with Orcas

Chris,
(1) BRIER is less common but not obsolete.
(2) You hated the Baywatch actress, I'd never heard of her. That happens even on Mondays. Luckily, this is a crossword.
@CCNY Your dad and a few others.

@Chungclan

Ha! 
You had me running back to
see what author I’d forgotten!

@Times Rita
I appreciated the honesty of the clue which did not treat USAID as an operating agency, instead looking at the corpse as the victim of the Musk-itoes

@Xword Junkie

Puzzle twins again!

@Xword Junkie
I always go with BRIAR first...born and bred, just like Brer Rabbit!  
Home was on Briarcliff Road near the intersection with North Druid Hills Rd.....There was plenty of "doggy-briar" around, you can bet, along with poison ivy.  My school was Briarcliff HS, 8-12th.
BRIER ....humph, looks mighty...fancy.
Crossworders should have a convention here: https://www.greenbrier.com/

@Xword Junkie Aha! We weren't sure which way the vowel would go til I checked the cross. I was surprised to see folks here so sure it would be an A-- but we're field ecologists and think about Smilax more often than briar patches of pastoral literature. I didn't realize the brier in greenbrier was a relict of older spelling.
@Mean Old Lady -Toco Hills! My husband and I met at Emory in 1979 and our first date was at Sambo's on N Druid Hills Rd.

@Erin + Erin

I'm just thrilled to be able to write Smilax and have it connect with anyone. ;-)

@Barry
My BIL has a conference at the Greenbrier next week. And we could have that meetup in the "secret" underground bunker.

@The X-Phile
It took me a minute or ten to parse out I'LL ALLOW IT - probably because it's a Down entry - but that W would not come to me. Oh duh, LOW cut, like a blouse. Sheesh!
It seems you do... https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/brier

@gen z maddy
The more familiar spelling of BriAr might help. We have briar roses, also known as dog roses. Blackberries are also briars.
@gen z Maddy this be got me as well. Brier - rare spelling I guess - crossed with a word I don’t really know how to spell. Otherwise very quick

@Dfitz 
But I feel so smart when I know the football clue. Then they have to throw a tennis clue in there and I lose my mind...

"It's a game!"

Dfitz,
That's the problem. The Crossword is supposed to be a puzzle, not a game. In the Magazine, it still says "Puzzles" (Edited by Will Shortz) but Puzzles is now part of Games.
It's not "custom-built" that some of us seek; we would like Games to let the Crossword go back to being a puzzle.

@Barry Ancona
I have to disagree on the "puzzles aren't a game" thing. I do think they're a game, it's just that I expect games to be actually challenging.

It's like if I'm playing a video game, and I'm coming up on a boss fight. I LEVEL UP, I make sure I have a bunch of healing potions, I equip my best gear. And then I beat the boss in less than a minute. The disappointment is huge.

That's how it feels to solve the NYT crossword recently. I've spent years improving my solving skills, and now they're all going to waste.

Katie,
It's semantics, but if we can't engage in wordplay here, all is truly lost. You and I want to "solve puzzles;" The Times is redirecting The Crossword to people who want to "play games."

@Dfitz

Hard to understand your reasoning. Whether it’s a game or puzzle, games have rules and people have to come to some agreement and it’s going be based on somebody’s preferences and way of thinking. They added a time clock to pitching in baseball—that was someone’s preference. They moved the kickoff line in football—that was a someone’s way of thinking. Some like it and some don’t and people debate about it. If the standards in the NYT puzzle change then we can debate about that too. The only real difference was for the other examples the debate was public, the rule changes were clear, and the stakeholders had some say in. It. In this case it seems that the standards are slowly changing without any official announcement or considering the needs of long time subscribers. Maybe another publication or outlet needs to take up the mantle for the most elite and challenging crosswords. Not sure who has the resources for it besides the NYT though.

@Dfitz

You're right.  It's just a game.  In the greater scheme of things, it doesn't matter very much. 
But, given the choice, wouldn't you rather have a good game?  a puzzling puzzle?
Once upon a time, we found them here.
In a world where harmless pleasures are hard to find, some of us are mourning what seems to be the passing of this one.

So, we'll take what pleasures we can find, but we'll continue to hope for better.

@Barry Ancona

I'd stay away from the "games are for fun only" theory.  Chess and tic-tac-toe are both games. There's nothing in the definition of a game that says it's either challenging or not challenging.

@SP

Maybe we can get Jeff Bezos to "save" the Washington Post crossword puzzle page.

@John
Ha! I can certainly relate to this.

@Mean Old Lady
I can still here my grandmother engaging in some serious Methodist swearing over that one.

@Lynn
My Granny bragged that she had never made a quilt (tsk)...and her version of Methodist swearing was "Ye gods and little fishes!"

"hear"
(oh, for an edit button)

@Mean Old Lady
Hmm, I replied with an example of my grandmother's Methodist swearing, but apparently even her substitutions for Biblical oaths were not allowed.

@Mean Old Lady 
Who is Shorty Jones, please?
Couldn't find him on the internet, unless he's that 1900s Pennsylvania gangster.
I do know of Smarty Jones, the race horse.
@ad absurdum The website code only allows me to "recommend" this comment once so I've come in here to recommend it again manually

@ad absurdum
so  by my count at 18:35 EDT  you have 36 recs. ;)

@Rich in Atlanta Oh, and one more puzzle find. This one rather surprising. A Sunday from February 13, 1977 by Elmer Toro with the title "Forgettable people." One list of theme answers: THESHERIFFOFNOTTINGHAM THEBELLMANOFLONDON THETHIEFOFBAGHDAD THEBARBEROFSEVILLE THEMAYOROFCASTERBRIDGE And what was quite surprising to me is that all of those answers were making their one and only appearance in any puzzle. And... four other answers that only ever appeared in ONE other puzzle: THEMERCHANTOFVENICE THEMASTEROFBALLANTRAE THEPIEDPIPEROFHAMELIN THEVICAROFWAKEFIELD There must be something I'm not understanding. Just really surprised by all of that. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=2/13/1977&g=90&d=A ....

@DIVAS IVLIVS
“Gob-smacked” comes to my mind (even if it means something entirely different).

@Charles Peterson
There have been some interesting LIE-INs. I think I participated in at least one in the 1990s. Our local ACT-UP and NOW chapters used to go to each other's protests and get pretty creative. The memories are vivid enough that both possible answers occurred to me at the same time so I waited on crosses to fill.

@Ken Burk
So...
notta lotta 
fun?

@Lynn I think it's always been that way. I've gradually built skills over the years, and my longest streaks have grown as well. I used to be very happy with a streak that lasted longer than a week. Now my streaks reach into the hundreds. Someday I'll hit a dead end, and then it's time for a new streak....

@Lynn

Great point. This whole streak then seems to be a marketing strategy—think Duolingo for example—but I think it’s only a short term fix. At some point you break your streak and then you say, ok, so what’s the point. I’d be delighted if that went away, but I know it’s a big deal for a lot of people. Honestly same with keeping track of your times, I think. It doesn’t allow people to savor puzzles and clues. I wish there were better strategies for motivating people than statistics, especially for crosswords that have so many variables and subjective elements.

@Dave Munger
Yes, your experience is like mine. We were accustomed to getting our streaks broken regularly with difficult puzzles. I think for folks who've been solving for only a year or so, their expectations have probably been set differently than if there had there been a traditionally difficult NYT puzzle every week or two.

@SP
I agree, I think it's a short term fix. I hope it was because the long term effect wasn't anticipated rather than that they didn't care.

@Lynn
I think you are wildly overestimating the ability of people who have been doing this for a year or so. Lots of these are hard for people who have not been doing it long.

@Gregory Dunn

Scramble and chuck the ball out of bounds!
@Gregory Dunn lifelong Cowboys fan here!
@Amy you’re a grandmother for sure
@Chad and love every second of it! 💕
@Amy three humblebrags in one brief comment must be a record, even in a forum known for them!
@Paul I thought it was extremely difficult
@Dave I was on the alert for that one. Recalled previous controversy, and reserved judgement.

@Fidelio
I can remember our HS band director calling out, "Elevator, elevator, pumpkin, pumpkin," to the percussion section.
@Fidelio The paradiddle rudiment far predates "Take Five." Check out The Company of Fifers and Drummers.

@Dale M

At least in the NYT puzzles, CZAR seems to be more business C-Suite related, while TSAR seems to be the go-to for Russian rulers.

@Robert

A breather is period of rest or inaction. So is a lull. The connotations are a little bit different but close enough.

@john ezra I had a similar reaction to MAILORDERBRIDE. It wasn’t nearly as cringy as another one from the archives, [Unpopular child’s name] and the answer was ADOLF. Yikes. 

But sometimes I think, maybe being made to feel a little uncomfortable isn’t such a bad thing.

@john ezra

Not only did Achilles know what was AFOOT, he was a LEG-END.

@Matthew S.
Yes, I had to flyspeck for a while because I was unfamiliar with this (horrible) alternate spelling.

@Matthew S. 
Thank you! Reading this comment fixed my one wrong square.

@Amy
"Cooking the books" refers to shady accounting. Most appropriate, just before tax day.

@Joe
I took it to be a reference to MLK.

@Tim It's an older slang meaning, but probably the only viable way of cluing it. Related to "bonehead," I think. There was a 1951 Batman comic where the Joker embarked on a whole crime spree themed on the term, in its meaning as clued here.

@J so I guess what you’re saying is I need to rethink every relationship I’ve ever had. Thanks, pal. As if I didn’t have enough keeping me up at night already! Now I’ve gotta wonder if it was actually my long-lasting *mistakes* that were keeping her around. And here I was starting to feel good about myself.
@David Bloom There’s no strict definition of “vegetable”
